title: docker-compose cannot pull images from authenticated private registry via tcp
body:
<!--
---------------------------------------------------
BUG REPORT INFORMATION
---------------------------------------------------
Use the commands below to provide key information from your environment:
You do NOT have to include this information if this is a FEATURE REQUEST

**NOTE** A large number of issues reported against Podman are often found to already be fixed
in more current versions of the project.  Before reporting an issue, please verify the
version you are running with `podman version` and compare it to the latest release
documented on the top of Podman's [README.md](../README.md).  If they differ, please
update your version of Podman to the latest possible and retry your command before creating
an issue.

Also, there is a running list of known issues in the [Podman Troubleshooting Guide](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/troubleshooting.md),
please reference that page before opening a new issue.

If you are filing a bug against `podman build`, please instead file a bug
against Buildah (https://github.com/containers/buildah/issues). Podman build
executes Buildah to perform container builds, and as such the Buildah
maintainers are best equipped to handle these bugs.
-->

**Is this a BUG REPORT or FEATURE REQUEST? (leave only one on its own line)**

/kind bug

**Description**

I'm using podman 4.2.0 on windows/wsl (installed according [to the tutorial(https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/docs/tutorials/podman-for-windows.md). Because of reasons based on our build-structure we're using a remote tcp instead of the socket when connecting docker-cli or docker-compose to podman (or formerly docker-engine). Thus i'm starting an additional instance of the podman-service listening on tcp via podman system service tcp:localhost:<port> -t 0

The images consumed and produced by our builds are stored in a private registry which is password protected.

When starting our containers using docker-compose it fails when it tries to pull a missing image from this registry even though authentication info is present in /run/user/<uid>/containers/auth.json after a successfull podman login. Running a remote pull using podman --url tcp://localhost:<port> pull <image> also works as expected.



**Steps to reproduce the issue:**

1. start a podman service listening to tcp (e.g. podman --log-level debug system service tcp:localhost:2375 -t 0)
2. create a docker-compose-file with an container build from an image in a password protected registry
3. run docker-compose up

**Describe the results you received:**

docker-compose up fails with the error-message  
initializing source docker://<registry>/<path>:<tag>: reading manifest latest in <registry>/<path>: unauthorized: unauthorized to access repository: <path>, action: pull: unauthorized to access repository: <path>, action: pull


**Describe the results you expected:**
docker-compose should pull the image as podman login was successfull

**Additional information you deem important (e.g. issue happens only occasionally):**
If ran the podman service with log-level debug and according to that the problem seems to be the credentials lookup is different for docker-compose up and e.g. podman -url <url> pull <image>

Log-Message when docker-compose up executes the pull:

```
DEBU[0036] Loading registries configuration "/etc/containers/registries.conf"
DEBU[0036] Loading registries configuration "/etc/containers/registries.conf.d/000-shortnames.conf"

DEBU[0036] IdleTracker:idle 2m+0h/4t connection(s)       X-Reference-Id=0xc000010058
```

A workaround seems to be to simply sym-link /run/users/<uid>/containers/auth.json to <user-home>/.config/containers/auth.json - this works for me for now but seems a bit hackish :-)


**Output of `podman version`:**

```
Client:       Podman Engine
Version:      4.2.0
API Version:  4.2.0
Go Version:   go1.18.4
Built:        Thu Aug 11 16:42:17 2022
OS/Arch:      linux/amd64
```

**Package info (e.g. output of `rpm -q podman` or `apt list podman`):**

```
(paste your output here)
```

**Have you tested with the latest version of Podman and have you checked the Podman Troubleshooting Guide? (https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/troubleshooting.md)**

Latest Version: No
Troubleshooting Guide: Yes

**Additional environment details (AWS, VirtualBox, physical, etc.):**
Windows 10, WSL2, besides the above changes a vanilla wsl2 distribution as created by podman machine init & start
docker-compose 2.10.2 (but did not work with a somewhat older 2.x-version, do not remember which one exactly)

comments.body:
I suspect that the XDG_RUNTIME_DIR env variable isn't set for the service. It remains unclear why that would be.

@mtrmac WDYT?
Okay. So if i export XDG_RUNTIME_DIR to /run/containers/users/$(id -u)/ explicitly it should work? I'll try that an provide feedback...

Starting podman service with:

```
/usr/bin/podman --runroot=/run/user/$(uid -u)/containers/ --log-level=debug system service tcp:localhost:2375 -t 0
```

does not make a difference. Setting the env var explicitly does neither:

```
/usr/bin/env XDG_RUTIME_DIR=/run/user/$(id -u)/containers /usr/bin/podman --log-level=debug system service tcp:localhost:2375 -t 0
```

As a `podman -url tcp://localhost:2375 pull ...` works this seems to be restricted to either a specific api-endpoint the (only) docker-compose uses and not that some setting is ignored for the whole service

> When starting our containers using docker-compose it fails when it tries to pull a missing image from this registry even though authentication info is present in /run/user//containers/auth.json after a successfull podman login. Running a remote pull using podman --url tcp://localhost: pull  also works as expected.

So the `auth.json` file exists on the client? And `docker-compose` is connecting over the API? I’d expect `docker-compose` to read config files and provide credentials over the API, then (it has to, if there are no server-side credentials).

I haven’t checked, but I wouldn’t expect `docker-compose` to be reading `containers/auth.json`. Does it work when placing the credentials to the traditional `.docker/config.json` file?

---

> I suspect that the XDG_RUNTIME_DIR env variable isn't set for the service. It remains unclear why that would be.

That should be visible in Podman’s logs, on the computer that is reading the config files. See the `No credentials matching <registry><path> found in` log entries. But I assume those are server-side logs, aren’t they?

`auth.json` exists in the container. When `docker-compose` calls the API via tcp, podman tries to find `auth.json` on/in the container, but instead of looking in `/run/user/<uid>/containers/auth.json` it looks in `<homedir>/.config/containers/auth.json` according to the logs. `podman pull` from the client via tcp (which also should use the API, shouldn'it) looks correctly for `/run/user<uid>/containers...` it seems. I've added the log-output of both cases in the issue's description, although the docker-compose is incomplete/intermingled with messages from other calls for the same compose-file.

These are the logs of the server - as docker-compose does call the API via tcp there should be no client involved - or should it? I'll have a look if there is a docker.exe somewhere still in the path on the client...

Symlinking `/run/user/<uid>/containers/auth.json` to `<homedir>/.config/containers/auth.json` resolves the problem (symlinking to `<homedir>/.docker/config.json` would work also, i assume... That's the reason i've opened the issue as it seems if not a problem on the `server-side` to be at least resolvable there, albeit quite hackish...

> In the container.

I don’t understand how containers figure into this. Is `docker-compose`, or the Podman server, running in a container of some kind?

---

> podman pull from the client via tcp (which also should use the API, shouldn'it) looks correctly for /run/user<uid>/containers... it seems. 

That’s not what I see happening _on the server_, per those logs. I _would_ expect the podman _client_ to do that, sure.

---

> as docker-compose does call the API via tcp there should be no client involved 

Well _something_ calls the server’s API. `docker-compose`, or something invoked by it, serves as a client to Podman’s server.

---

Or maybe I’m completely wrong about the location of credentials in the client-server scenario??

Still, on success
```
Found credentials for <registry><path> in credential helper containers-auth.json in file /var/tmp/auth.json.1906286197
```
(_not_ the primary `auth.json`, a temporary single-use file), I think, suggests use of https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/8882b7664d225bf7f8efac6ab10e8d583e48c712/pkg/auth/auth.go#L236 , i.e. that the credentials come from the client as part of the API request.

> , i.e. that the credentials come from the client as part of the API request.

Great catch.  So it works with the podman _client_ as it sends the credentials in the request. `docker-compose` does not see these credentials.

Why does symlinking to `<homedir>/.config/containers/auth.json` work?

Oh. Yes, sorry, i executed the `podman --url tcp://localhost:2375 pull <image>` in the wsl2-distribution that time, sorry (had tried it in many different ways that day - either in the distribution or from the client). 

When executing podman --url in the distribution/server, it seems to send the credentials from auth.json (as does the podman-call from the client with the credentials saved there), but docker-compose (and/or the docker-cli in docker.exe i've found on the  client in the meantime) does not send them (as it looks for the docker-config-files which do not exist on the client). 

In that case the service seems to try to look for credentials stored on the server - which is the reason symlinking kinda "fixes" the problem. 

So basically what i'll have to do is somehow tell docker-compose to use the podman on the client instead of the docker.exe and then it should work ich the podman on the client is logged into the registry, right?

> So basically what i'll have to do is somehow tell docker-compose to use the podman on the client instead of the docker.exe and then it should work ich the podman on the client is logged into the registry, right?

I would try using `podman login --authfile $WhatDockerComposeWants`.  This way, docker-compose can read the authfile from where it wants, which very likely is `<homedir>/.docker/config.json`.

When doing that on the client with an empty/non-existing file, podman complains that the registry (or any registry?) is not known:

```
podman login --authfile C:\Users\<user>\.docker\config.json
Error: no registries found in registries.conf, a registry must be provided
```
Is that correct? Why does podman (on the client) expect the registries.conf on the client when using --authfile but uses the configuration form the server/inside the wsl2-distribution when executing the login without the `--authfile`-parameter?

`podman login --authfile C:\Users\<user>\.docker\config.json`


Did you specify which registry you want to login to?

Whaa. Sorry, been responding here while working through our own support-tickets and -messages at work - never a good idea :-/ 

When running `podman login --authfile C:\Users\<user>\.docker\config.json <registry>` the command works without error-message. But it simply ignores the `--authfile`-parameter as long as `C:\Users\<user>\.config\containers\auth.json` contains an entry for the registry. After removing that file and logging in with `--authfile`, docker-compose works as expected!
Excellent, thanks for confirming!  I will create a follow-up issue for the --authfile issue.
